audits.title: Render-blocking requests
audits.description: Requests are blocking the page's initial render, which may delay LCP. can move these network requests out of the critical path.

audits.title: Legacy JavaScript
audits.description: Polyfills and transforms enable older browsers to use new JavaScript features. However, many aren't necessary for modern browsers. Consider modifying your JavaScript build process to not transpile features, unless you know you must support older browsers.

audits.title: Speed Index
audits.description: Speed Index shows how quickly the contents of a page are visibly populated..

audits.title: Duplicated JavaScript
audits.description: Remove large, from bundles to reduce unnecessary bytes consumed by network activity.

audits.title: SVG elements with an `img` role do not have an accessible text alternative
audits.description: Ensures SVG elements with an `img`, `graphics-document` or `graphics-symbol` role have an accessible text alternative..

audits.title: Missing source maps for large first-party JavaScript
audits.description: Source maps translate minified code to the original source code. This helps developers debug in production. In addition, Lighthouse is able to provide further insights. Consider deploying source maps to take advantage of these benefits..

audits.title: Max Potential First Input Delay
audits.description: The maximum potential First Input Delay that your users could experience is the duration of the longest task..

audits.title: Avoid enormous network payloads
audits.description: Large network payloads cost users real money and are highly correlated with long load times..

audits.title: Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio.
audits.description: Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read..

audits.title: Network dependency tree
audits.description: by reducing the length of chains, reducing the download size of resources, or deferring the download of unnecessary resources to improve page load.

audits.title: Links do not have descriptive text
audits.description: Descriptive link text helps search engines understand your content..

audits.title: Largest Contentful Paint
audits.description: Largest Contentful Paint marks the time at which the largest text or image is painted.

audits.title: Improve image delivery
audits.description: Reducing the download time of images can improve the perceived load time of the page and LCP.

audits.title: Reduce unused CSS
audits.description: Reduce unused rules from stylesheets and defer CSS not used for above-the-fold content to decrease bytes consumed by network activity..

audits.title: Buttons do not have an accessible name
audits.description: When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers..

audits.title: Total Blocking Time
audits.description: Sum of all time periods between FCP and Time to Interactive, when task length exceeded 50ms, expressed in milliseconds..

audits.title: LCP request discovery
audits.description: by making the LCP image discoverable from the HTML immediately, and avoiding lazy-loading

audits.title: Time to Interactive
audits.description: Time to Interactive is the amount of time it takes for the page to become fully interactive..

audits.title: Use efficient cache lifetimes
audits.description: A long cache lifetime can speed up repeat visits to your page..

audits.title: Reduce unused JavaScript
audits.description: Reduce unused JavaScript and defer loading scripts until they are required to decrease bytes consumed by network activity..

rules.description: Disclosure of paid testimonials / endorsements
Consumer Rights Act statutory information
Pricing transparency: total price including unavoidable fees
Subscription traders must send reminders before renewal and offer one-click cancellation.
Customer-facing websites must offer reasonable adjustments under the Equality Act 2010.
Identity of the controller must be disclosed on first data collection page
Contact details of the controller (including DPO if applicable)
Purposes of processing AND legal basis
International transfers and safeguards (SCCs / adequacy)
Retention period or criteria
Right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, port, object
Right to withdraw consent (if consent is the basis)
Right to complain to ICO
Analytics cookies require consent (ICO position 2023)
Cookie banner must not use pre-ticked boxes
Cookie wall (pay-or-OK) for unrelated content is not freely-given consent
In-scope user-to-user services must publish illegal-content risk assessments and protections.
Services must provide easy-to-use reporting and complaint channels for harmful content.
Soft opt-in: prior similar product/service customer relationship for marketing
Marketing emails must include sender identity (no false header)
Marketing emails must include valid return address
Easy opt-out mechanism in every marketing email
Cookie consent before non-essential cookies set
Cookie reject must be as prominent as accept
Cookie purpose disclosed clearly before consent
Business identification on website (Co. No, VAT, Address)
Author byline on substantive content (Experience + Expertise)
Author credentials visible (Authoritativeness)
Honest claims (no superlatives without substantiation)
Pricing transparency (incl. VAT or excluding clearly stated)
Honest reviews disclosure (no fake or paid reviews)
All-in pricing, no hidden mandatory fees (DMCC drip-pricing ban)
UK limited companies must display registered name, number, registered office on every website.
Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires clear rights notice on commercial websites.
Total price including mandatory fees must be displayed up-front, not added at checkout.
Must publish a policy declaring no incentivised, fake or undisclosed reviews.
Reviews or testimonials shown without disclosing incentives or a genuine-review verification process
Clear subscription terms + easy cancellation (DMCC)
Section 3 personal data definition disclosure
Legitimate interests (if relied on) clearly stated
Recipients or categories of recipients of personal data
Whether provision is statutory/contractual and consequences of not providing
Existence of automated decision-making including profiling
Cookie purposes must be itemised (functional vs analytics vs marketing)
Cookie list must include third-party cookies
Cookie banner copy must be available before clicking accept
Cookie consent log must be retained for 12 months minimum
Re-prompt for consent after material changes to cookie usage
Marketing or analytics trackers present but no consent-management platform gating them before consent
Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All
B2B corporate subscriber: soft opt-in still required for individual employees identifiable in role
Cookie withdrawal mechanism accessible from every page
Unit pricing for pre-packaged goods
Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)
Published content shows author / expertise signals (E-E-A-T)
Substantiated, non-misleading environmental/green claims
No false urgency / misleading countdown timers
Incorporated entity not displaying company number, registered office and place of registration on its website
VAT-registered businesses must show VAT number on website per HMRC guidance.
Online sales with no visible 14-day cancellation, returns or refund rights for distance consumers
Fair, transparent and legible consumer contract terms
Age-appropriate design (ICO Children Code)
Section 22 manual unstructured data exemption noted where applicable
Image alt text as a reasonable adjustment
UK right of access (Subject Access Request)
UK right to erasure
UK right to data portability
UK right to object to direct marketing
UK processor / sub-processor transparency
UK records of processing (ROPA)
UK lawful basis identified
Cookie scan must distinguish session vs persistent cookies
Cookie banner not shown to authenticated users with stored preferences
Age assurance + illegal-content safety duties (Online Safety Act)
Valid marketing consent or soft opt-in for email/SMS
Strictly necessary cookies do not require consent but must be disclosed
Distance-selling pre-contract information (CCRs 2013)
VAT number displayed where VAT-registered
Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration
Clear price marking incl. unit/total price

terms.da: Domain Authority: a 0 to 100 trust score based on the quality of sites linking to you. The higher it is, the easier everything else ranks.
terms.pa: Page Authority: the same trust idea as Domain Authority but for one specific page rather than the whole site.
terms.cls: Cumulative Layout Shift: how much your page jumps around while loading. Jumpy pages frustrate users and rank worse.
terms.csp: Content-Security-Policy: a security header that blocks malicious scripts. Security reviews check for it.
terms.geo: Generative Engine Optimisation: whether AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) actually name and cite your business when a buyer asks them for a provider like you.
terms.lcp: Largest Contentful Paint: how long your main content takes to appear. Over 2.5 seconds and Google ranks you lower and visitors leave.
terms.nap: Name, Address, Phone. Search engines cross-check these to trust that your business is real and local. Inconsistent or missing NAP sinks local rankings.
terms.ccpa: California's privacy law, and the template for the roughly 20 US states that now have one. It gives consumers the right to opt out and to have data deleted.
terms.gdpr: The data-protection law for the UK and EU. Fines reach GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover, so gaps here are the most expensive on the page.
terms.hsts: A security header that stops your site being downgraded from secure HTTPS. Corporate buyers check for it, so its absence is an easy red flag.
terms.pecr: UK rules on cookies and electronic marketing. You must get clear consent before non-essential cookies or trackers fire.
terms.sameas: Schema links that connect your site to your verified profiles (LinkedIn, Companies House, Wikidata) so AI knows the business is really you.
terms.schema: Hidden code (structured data) that tells search engines and AI exactly who you are, what you offer and where. Without it they have to guess, and they guess wrong.
terms.e-e-a-t: Google's test of Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness and Trust. It decides whether your content deserves to rank, and it matters most in law, health and finance.
terms.organic: The unpaid search results, as opposed to ads. Ranking here is the durable, compounding kind of visibility.
terms.uk gdpr: The UK version of the EU data-protection law, enforced by the ICO with fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover.
terms.llms.txt: A simple file that tells AI models what your site is about and what they may use. It is a new standard and most firms do not have one yet, so adding it is an easy edge.
terms.map pack: The three-business map result at the top of local searches. The top spots take the majority of local clicks.
terms.wikidata: Google's and AI's open knowledge base. An entry there makes you a recognised entity that answer engines can confidently cite.
terms.canonical: A line of code that tells Google which version of a page is the real one, so duplicate web addresses do not split your ranking power.
terms.ai crawler: The automated readers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended) that AI answer engines send to read your site. Block them and those engines cannot cite you.
terms.local pack: The three businesses Google shows on a small map at the top of local searches. Most local clicks go to those three, so being outside it costs real enquiries.
terms.robots.txt: A file that tells web crawlers which parts of your site they may read. One wrong line here can make you invisible to Google or to AI engines.
terms.ai visibility: Whether the AI answer engines buyers now ask first will name and cite you, or only your competitors.
terms.share of voice: How often you appear versus your competitors when buyers search or ask AI in your category. Low share of voice means they are heard and you are not.
terms.structured data: Hidden code that spells out who you are and what you offer so Google and AI can read it precisely instead of guessing.
terms.domain authority: A 0 to 100 score of how much trust your site has earned from links on other websites. Higher means Google and AI engines are more likely to rank and cite you. It is the single biggest reason one site outranks another.
terms.meta description: The summary Google shows under your result. A good one lifts click-through; if it is missing, Google writes its own from random page text.

news_map.UK_CMA: CMA (2026-05-28): Court endorses CMA action as Emma Sleep agrees to change sales practices. Source: https://www.gov.uk/government/news/court-endorses-cma-action-as-emma-sleep-agrees-to-change-sales-practices
news_map.UK_PECR: The DUAA came into force 5 Feb 2026, raising the maximum PECR fine to GBP 17.5M (from GBP 500k); the ICO is reviewing the UK top 1,000 websites cookie banners.
news_map.UK_ASA_CAP: The ASA AI monitoring banned ads in 2025 for greenwashing (Wizz Air), misleading product claims (Origin Mattress) and false-urgency countdown timers (Oct 2025).
news_map.GOOGLE_EEAT: Google March 2024 core update emphasised E-E-A-T; sites without author bylines saw 31% traffic drop.
news_map.UK_CRA_2015: CMA confirms CRA 2015 applies in parallel with DMCC. Cross-referenced in 2025 enforcement.
news_map.UK_DPA_2018: ICO 2025 enforcement hit GBP 19.6M from 7 cases (vs GBP 2.7M in 2024); the DUAA came into force 5 Feb 2026 with new compulsion powers.
news_map.UK_GDPR_A13: ICO issued GBP 19.6M across 7 cases in 2025 (Capita GBP 14M, Advanced Computer Software GBP 3.07M, 23andMe GBP 2.31M, LastPass GBP 1.23M); two-thirds were UK GDPR breaches.
news_map.UK_OSA_2023: Ofcom Phase 1 illegal-content codes March 2025. Fines up to £18M or 10% global turnover.
news_map.UK_DMCC_2024: CMA gained direct fining powers up to 10% global turnover from April 2025.
news_map.UK_ICO_COOKIES: DUAA (in force 5 Feb 2026) lifts PECR fines to GBP 17.5M; the ICO is actively reviewing the top 1,000 UK sites and warned non-compliant cookie banners.
news_map.UK_COMPANIES_ACT: Companies House active enforcement of website disclosure post Economic Crime Act 2023.
news_map.UK_EQUALITY_2010: EHRC 2024 digital-accessibility code. Damages claims up 18% in 2024.

pointers.fact: Absent from the AI / search answer surface for "online shopping platform UK"

bingo.fix: Tamazia runs the GEO + entity programme (Schema.org, llms.txt, a Wikidata entity and authoritative content) that puts you into the set of sources AI engines read and cite for your category.
bingo.problem: Right now: Absent from the AI / search answer surface for "online shopping platform UK".

pointers.evidence: live SERP: online shopping platform UK
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia runs the GEO + entity programme (Schema.org, llms.txt, a Wikidata entity and authoritative content) that puts you into the set of sources AI engines read and cite for your category.
pointers.layman_explanation: When a buyer asks ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI or a search engine for "online shopping platform UK", your site is not in the top 9 results these engines read and cite. The firms that own that answer surface today are ecommerceguide.com, metricscart.com, merchantmachine.co.uk. AI engines synthesise answers from these ranked, recognised sources, so they name your competitors and not you.

pointers.fact: For "best online shopping platform in india" you are not visible in the top 100; ecommerceguide.com owns position #1.

bingo.fix: Tamazia builds the page depth and authority to move "best online shopping platform in india" into the top three, where the clicks and the AI citations are.
bingo.problem: Right now: For "best online shopping platform in india" you are not visible in the top 100; ecommerceguide.com owns position #1. Here it is on your site: “live SERP: you not visible in the top 100 vs ecommerceguide.com #1”.

pointers.evidence: live SERP (free-serp) · query "best online shopping platform in india"
pointers.evidence_quote: live SERP: you not visible in the top 100 vs ecommerceguide.com #1
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the page depth and authority to move "best online shopping platform in india" into the top three, where the clicks and the AI citations are.
pointers.layman_explanation: When your buyers search "best online shopping platform in india", they find ecommerceguide.com, not you. Page two and below is effectively zero clicks, so this exact query hands its high-intent traffic to a competitor every day, and AI answers cite the top-ranked pages, so being invisible here also keeps you out of the AI results. The firms winning your buyer queries: forbes.com, ecommerceguide.com.

pointers.fact: Your AI entity-readiness score is 57/100: no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt.

bingo.fix: Tamazia builds your machine-readable entity: full Organization schema with sameAs, a Wikidata entry, and an llms.txt, so the answer engines can identify and cite you.
bingo.problem: Right now: Your AI entity-readiness score is 57/100: no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt. Here it is on your site: “no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt”.

pointers.evidence_quote: no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds your machine-readable entity: full Organization schema with sameAs, a Wikidata entry, and an llms.txt, so the answer engines can identify and cite you.
pointers.layman_explanation: AI answer engines decide who to cite by first identifying you as a known, trusted entity. They read your Organization schema, the sameAs links that connect you to your verified profiles, and knowledge bases like Wikidata. You are missing 4 of these anchors, so to an AI engine you are an unknown string rather than a citable provider, and it names the firms it can identify instead.

pointers.fact: Asked "online shopping platform UK" 2 times, an AI engine named you in 0 of 2 answers; it named Amazon (2/2), eBay (2/2), ASOS (2/2).

bingo.fix: Tamazia builds the entity, content and authority signals that get you named consistently when buyers ask AI for a provider like you.
bingo.problem: Right now: Asked "online shopping platform UK" 2 times, an AI engine named you in 0 of 2 answers; it named Amazon (2/2), eBay (2/2), ASOS (2/2). Here it is on your site: “you 0/2 vs Amazon 2/2”.

pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the entity, content and authority signals that get you named consistently when buyers ask AI for a provider like you.
pointers.layman_explanation: AI answers vary run to run, so we asked 2 times (via nvidia-nim). You were never named, while Amazon appeared 2 of 2. The firms named every time are the ones AI now treats as the default answer for your buyers, compounding while you are absent.

pointers.fact: Asked who you are by name, a leading AI model has no reliable information about your firm.

bingo.fix: Tamazia builds the entity footprint (schema, Wikidata, authoritative mentions) that makes AI models actually know and describe you correctly.
bingo.problem: Right now: Asked who you are by name, a leading AI model has no reliable information about your firm. Here it is on your site: “model reply: "NO RELIABLE INFO."”.

pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the entity footprint (schema, Wikidata, authoritative mentions) that makes AI models actually know and describe you correctly.
pointers.layman_explanation: When a buyer asks an AI assistant about you by name, it cannot describe you, so it will not vouch for or recommend you, and when pushed it may invent details (a hallucination) that you cannot control. Competitors the model does know get described and recommended. Becoming a model-known entity is what fixes this.

bingo.fix: Tamazia sets canonical tags site-wide to consolidate authority.
bingo.problem: Right now: No canonical URL declared.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia sets canonical tags site-wide to consolidate authority.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia sets canonical tags site-wide to consolidate authority.
pointers.layman_explanation: Without a canonical tag, duplicate URL variants (www, trailing slash, query strings) compete against each other and split your ranking authority.

bingo.fix: Tamazia enables HSTS at the edge.
bingo.problem: Right now: No HTTP Strict-Transport-Security header.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia enables HSTS at the edge.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia enables HSTS at the edge.
pointers.layman_explanation: Connections can be downgraded from HTTPS, and enterprise procurement and security questionnaires routinely check for HSTS. Its absence is an avoidable trust gap with corporate clients.

pointers.layman_explanation: A long cache lifetime can speed up repeat visits to your page.. Measured live by Google PageSpeed Insights on your mobile site.

bingo.fix: Tamazia strips unused JavaScript from the critical path.
bingo.problem: Right now: Reduce unused JavaScript.

pointers.evidence: Google PageSpeed (mobile) · unused-javascript · Est savings of 293 KiB
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia strips unused JavaScript from the critical path.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia strips unused JavaScript from the critical path.
pointers.layman_explanation: Reduce unused JavaScript and defer loading scripts until they are required to decrease bytes consumed by network activity.. Measured live by Google PageSpeed Insights on your mobile site.

bingo.fix: Tamazia generates and submits an XML sitemap.
bingo.problem: Right now: No sitemap.xml found.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia generates and submits an XML sitemap.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia generates and submits an XML sitemap.
pointers.layman_explanation: Without a sitemap, search engines discover pages slowly and may miss deep content entirely.

bingo.fix: Tamazia publishes a curated llms.txt.
bingo.problem: Right now: No llms.txt file.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia publishes a curated llms.txt.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia publishes a curated llms.txt.
pointers.layman_explanation: llms.txt is the emerging standard that tells AI assistants how to represent your firm. Publishing one puts you ahead of peers who have not.

pointers.fact: DMARC is set to p=none (monitor only, not enforcing).

bingo.fix: Tamazia moves you to p=quarantine then p=reject after monitoring.
bingo.problem: Right now: DMARC is set to p=none (monitor only, not enforcing).

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia moves you to p=quarantine then p=reject after monitoring.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia moves you to p=quarantine then p=reject after monitoring.
pointers.layman_explanation: You can see spoofing reports but receivers are not told to reject impostor mail, so the protection is not actually active.

pointers.fact: Your Domain Authority is 31/100. Your top 2 competitors: forbes.com 48/100, ecommerceguide.com 37/100.

bingo.fix: Tamazia runs the digital-PR and authority-building programme (earned links, citations, entity coverage) that lifts your Domain Authority toward the firms outranking you.
bingo.problem: Right now: Your Domain Authority is 31/100. Your top 2 competitors: forbes.com 48/100, ecommerceguide.com 37/100. Here it is on your site: “you 31/100 (global rank 4,098,814) vs forbes.com 48/100, ecommerceguide.com 37/100”.

pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia runs the digital-PR and authority-building programme (earned links, citations, entity coverage) that lifts your Domain Authority toward the firms outranking you.
pointers.layman_explanation: Domain Authority is the backlink-trust score (here on a 0 to 100 scale) that Google and AI engines use to decide who to rank and cite. forbes.com carries 1.5 times of it than you, which is the structural reason these firms sit above you for your buyer queries and get cited in AI answers while you do not. Closing a rank gap without closing the authority gap rarely holds.

pointers.fact: Your domain is not signed with DNSSEC.

bingo.fix: Tamazia enables DNSSEC at your DNS provider so every lookup of your domain is cryptographically verified.
bingo.problem: Right now: Your domain is not signed with DNSSEC.

pointers.evidence: DNS · no DS record and AD flag false
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia enables DNSSEC at your DNS provider so every lookup of your domain is cryptographically verified.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia enables DNSSEC at your DNS provider so every lookup of your domain is cryptographically verified.
pointers.layman_explanation: Without DNSSEC, attackers can forge DNS responses (cache poisoning) to silently redirect your visitors or intercept email. Enterprise, finance and government client security reviews increasingly require it.

keywords.keyword: best online shopping platform in india

keyword_leaders.keyword: best online shopping platform in india

regimes.why: applies to every site that wants to rank in Google or be cited by AI answer engines, regardless of where it is registered

jurisdiction_statement.statement: loaf is registered in the United Kingdom and its own website shows it serves clients in the UK. It is therefore bound by UK law (UK GDPR, PECR, CMA/DMCC, sector regulators), not only the law of its country of registration. This audit applies the laws of every jurisdiction the site shows you operate in, and no others.
